## PAGE 00001: (2026x) Accept action

- page_id: `298093835`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093835/2026x+Accept+action
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:22:04.910+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions > (2026x) Transfers between actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An accept action usage is declared as an action usage whose behavior is to accept the transfer of a payload received by the given receiver, and then output that payload. It has two parameters:

1. A parameter for a set of payload values , identified by the *accept* keyword.
  1. The payload parameter declaration for an accept action usage identifies the type of values accepted by the accept action.
  2. A payload parameter declaration can include a feature value (change trigger, absolute time trigger, relative time trigger). In this case, the accept action usage will only accept exactly the value that is the result of the feature value expression.
2. An input parameter giving a receiver occurrence , identified by the *via* keyword.

**Accepting context**

- If an accept action usage is a composite feature of a part definition or usage, then the default for the receiver ( via ) of the accept action usage is the containing part, not the accept action itself.
- If an accept action usage is not a composite feature of a part definition or usage, then the default for the receiver ( via ) of the accept action usage is the highest-level containing action usage.
- If an accept action usage is contained by a perform action, then the default for the receiver ( via ) of the accept action usage is the perform action, not the part or the higher-level action.
- When accepting through a port, the port usage is the receiver ( via ).

##### Specifying accept actions for actions

###### Specifying accept actions for actions via the Textual Editor

To specify accept actions for actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the send action and do one of the following:
  1. For a named accept action usage, declare the keyword action , specify the element name, and then the keyword accept .
  2. For an unnamed accept action usage, declare the keyword accept .
2. Follow with the expression for the payload.
3. (Optional) Specify a feature value of a payload parameter:
  1. A change trigger with the keyword when followed by an expression whose result must be a Boolean value.
  2. An absolute time trigger with the keyword at followed by an expression whose result must be a TimeInstantValue.
  3. A relative time trigger with the keyword after followed by an expression whose result must be a DurationValue.
4. (Optional) Follow with the keyword via and the expression for the receiver.
5. Click the Synchronize button.

// ------------ accepting without ports; the receiver is the part 'camera'

partcamera:Camera{ 
actiontakePicture:TakePicture{

// named accept action 'trigger' accepting the transfer of the payload 'scene' by the receiver 'camera': 
actiontriggeracceptscene:Scene; 
// unnamed accept action usage:acceptscene:Scene; 
// accept action usage with apayload parameter's change trigger feature value:actiontriggeracceptscene:ScenewhennormalTemp<maxTemp;// accept action usage with apayload parameter'sabsolute time trigger feature value: 
actiontriggeracceptscene:SceneatcurrentTime; // accept action usage with apayload parameter'srelative time trigger feature value: 
actiontriggeracceptscene:Sceneafter5[s];

thenactionfocus:Focus{ 
inscene=trigger.scene; 
outimage; 
} 
flowfocus.imagetoshoot.image; 
thenactionshoot:Shoot{ 
inimage; 
outpicture; 
} 
thenactiondisplayPicturesendshoot.picturetoscreen; 
} 
}

// ------------ accepting through ports; the receiver is the port 'viewPort'

partcamera:Camera{ 
portviewPort; 
portdisplayPort;actiontakePicture:TakePicture{

// named accept action 'trigger' accepting the transfer of the payload 'scene' by the port 'viewPort': 
actiontriggeracceptscene:SceneviaviewPort; 
// unnamed accept action usage: 
 accept scene : Scene via viewPort; // accept action usage with apayload parameter's change trigger feature value:actiontriggeracceptscene:ScenewhennormalTemp<maxTempviaviewPort;// accept action usage with apayload parameter'sabsolute time trigger feature value: 
actiontriggeracceptscene:SceneatcurrentTimeviaviewPort; // accept action usage with apayload parameter'srelative time trigger feature value: 
actiontriggeracceptscene:Sceneafter5[s]viaviewPort;

thenactionfocus:Focus{ 
inscene=trigger.scene; 
outimage; 
} 
flowfocus.imagetoshoot.image; 
thenactionshoot:Shoot{ 
inimage; 
outpicture; 
}thenactiondisplayPicturesendshoot.picturevia displayPort; 
} 
}

###### Specifying accept actions for actions via the view palette

To specify accept actions for actions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the accept button to create an accept action usage element.
2. Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the accept symbol.
3. (Optional) Specify the element's parameters via the Textual Editor.

create an accept action usage element.</li><li>Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the accept symbol.</li><li>(Optional)<span> </span>Specify the element's parameters via the Textual Editor.</li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093887 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00002: (2026x) Action decomposition

- page_id: `298093887`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093887/2026x+Action+decomposition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:22:41.752+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Action definitions and action usages can be decomposed into lower-level actions to create an action tree. You can define an action usage by an action definition, and an action usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of action usages to adapt to its context.

##### Decomposing action definitions

You can represent the next level of decomposition of an action definition using the feature typing (*defined by*) relationship.

###### Decomposing action definitions via the Textual Editor

To decompose action definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the action usage that you want to define, nested within an action definition.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Feature typing' space='UAF2P'] .

actiondefDelivery{ 
actionloadCargo:LoadCargo;// action usage, owned by an action definition, defined by another action definition 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverCargo:DeliverCargo; 
} 
actiondefLoadCargo; 
actiondefTakeOff; 
actiondefDeliverCargo;

###### Displaying decompositions of action definitions in a view

To display decompositions of action definitions in a view

- In a view, select the action definition symbol and the action usage symbol it defines, then use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The feature typing relationship is displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Decomposing action usages

You can display an action tree representing a composition of an action from other actions.

###### Decomposing action usages via the Textual Editor

To decompose action usages via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the action you want to decompose, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space='UAF2P'] .
2. Click the Synchronize button.

action'package delivery':Delivery{ // the owning action usage with nested action usages 
actionloadPackage:LoadCargo; 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverPackge:DeliverCargo; 
}

###### Decomposing action usages via the view palette by creating a composite action

To decompose action usages via the view palette by creating a composite action

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the action for which you want to create a composite action.
2. Create a composite action in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (action symbol option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the action button to create an action usage element.
    2. Click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane to create an action owned by another.
  2. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create an action owned by another.

###### Decomposing action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one

To decompose action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the actions you want to designate as the owner and the owned.
2. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
3. First, click on the action symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.

###### Displaying decompositions of action usages in a view

To display decompositions of action usages in a view

- In a view, select the owning action usage symbol and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the Tree-View Style category. The feature membership and the owned features (action usages) are displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Reusing and modifying the action tree

An action can subset another action, facilitating action tree reuse and modification. The subsetting action inherits the action decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited actions and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new actions.

###### Reusing and modifying the actions tree via the Textual Editor

To reuse and modify the actions tree via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space='UAF2P'] you want to reuse, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space='UAF2P'] it.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Redefinition' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space='UAF2P'] the action features in the element body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

action'package delivery' :Delivery{// subsetted action 
actionloadPackage:LoadCargo; 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverPackge:DeliverCargo; 
} 
action'heater delivery':>'package delivery'{// an action reuses the actions tree by subsetting another action 
action:>>loadPackage; 
action:>>takeOff; 
action:>>deliverPackge; 
}

###### Reusing the actions tree via the view palette

To reuse the actions tree via the view palette

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] the action whose decomposition you want to reuse.
2. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the subsetting button.
3. Click on the action you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the action you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.

###### Displaying the actions tree reuse in a view

To display the actions tree reuse in a view

1. In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted action usage symbols, then u se the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The subsetting relationship is displayed.
2. (Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted actions, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'] command. The owned features (action usages) are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='Displaying the actions tree reuse on a diagram.png']

definitions in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the action definition symbol and the action usage symbol it defines, then use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Specializations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command under the <strong>In-Between Relationships category</strong>. <br />The feature typing relationship is displayed.</li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying decompositions of action definitions on a diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Action decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Decomposing action usages</h3><p>You can display an action tree representing a composition of an action from other actions.</p><h4>Decomposing action usages via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To decompose action usages via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">In the Textual Editor, within the body of the action you want to decompose, </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create the new action usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="698a77ad-c59e-4286-9cdd-a7902f3f2cf1"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the action for which you want to create a composite action.</li><li>Create a composite action in one of the following ways:<ol><li>Via the view palette (action symbol option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the <strong>action</strong><span> button to create an action usage element.</span></li><li>Click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane to create an action owned by another. </li></ol></li><li>Via the view palette (feature membership option):<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create an action owned by another.</li></ol></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Decomposing action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one</h4><p>To decompose action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one</p><hr /><ol><li>In a view, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the actions you want to designate as the owner and the owned.</li><li>In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the <strong>feature membership</strong> <span>button.</span></li><li>First, click on the action symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.</li></ol><h4>Displaying decompositions of action usages in a view</h4><p>To display decompositions of action usages in a view</p><hr /><ul><li>In a view, select the owning action usage symbol and use the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>command under the <strong>Tree-View Style</strong> category. <br />The feature membership and the owned features (action usages) are displayed. </li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Displaying decompositions of action usages on a diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Action decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Reusing and modifying the action tree</h3><p>An action can subset another action, facilitating action tree reuse and modification. The subsetting action inherits the action decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited actions and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new actions.</p><h4>Reusing and modifying the actions tree via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To reuse and modify the actions tree via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[declare the action usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you want to reuse, then <ac:link ac:anchor="Subsetting a usage via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> it.</li><li>(Optional) <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Redefinition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Redefine]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the action features in the element body.</li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8811e49e-ed80-4671-a9c5-db96a9276d25"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093815 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00003: (2026x) Action succession and flow

- page_id: `298093815`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093815/2026x+Action+succession+and+flow
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-11T19:46:59.125+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can connect actions using successions and input/output flows.

50%

Use successions to connect and sequence actions:

- A succession asserts that the target action can start execution only after the source action ends execution.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Succession' space='UAF2P'] page.
- A succession within the body of an action definition or usage may be given a guard condition represented as a Boolean expression, forming a conditional succession . A conditional succession asserts that the target action can start only if the guard condition is true .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Guard conditions' space='UAF2P'] page.

In addition to the regular full notation for successions, you can also use textual notation succession shorthands within the body of an action definition or usage. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Succession' space='UAF2P'] page.

50%

Use the following kinds of input/output flows to connect elements and transfer items from a source output to a target input:

- A flow is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute (this is the default)
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Flow' space='UAF2P'] page.
- A succession flow is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Succession flow' space='UAF2P'] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can connect actions using successions and input/output flows.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="section" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f84a348-430c-4c9d-8dea-e2cf87878dfc"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d02806b8-f825-420f-adc8-c87b134cef4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Use successions to connect and sequence actions:</span></p><ul><li data-uuid="ba426313-dd3c-4e1d-9e04-aeb00c329f0a"><span>A </span><strong>succession</strong><span> asserts that the target action can start execution only after the source action ends execution. </span><ul><li><span>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Succession" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Succession]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</span></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="025ff679-ef8d-48cb-8c63-1b56c858771a">A succession within the body of an action definition or usage may be given a guard condition represented as a Boolean expression, forming a <strong>conditional succession</strong>. A conditional succession asserts that the target action can start only if the guard condition is <em>true</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Guard conditions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Guard conditions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c724e1f3-91b2-4c79-b12f-6c78400b9010"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>In addition to the regular full notation for successions, you can also use textual notation succession shorthands within the body of an action definition or usage. See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Succession" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Succession]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a3d1cd64-5744-4722-bf82-9b2bed2efbc4"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Use the following kinds of input/output flows to connect elements and transfer items from a source output to a target input: </span></p><ul><li data-uuid="19356704-8df9-4b21-b0d4-bb180c4c5bf6"><span>A </span><strong>flow</strong><span> is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute (this is the default) </span><ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li data-uuid="4175f624-b57d-4d6c-8ba9-59d982b37066">A <strong>succession flow </strong>is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Succession flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Succession flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298094132 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00005: (2026x) Activating style sheets

- page_id: `298094132`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298094132/2026x+Activating+style+sheets
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:27:08.283+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) View > (2026x) Symbol styles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Whether you're using predefined style sheets provided by the modeling tool or custom ones you've created, you can manage which style sheets are activated for the entire project and which for specific views. This flexibility allows you to combine multiple style sheets based on your modeling needs—for example, activating a global style sheet for the entire project while activating distinct style sheets for individual views. Additionally, style sheets activated at both the project and view levels can be prioritized by setting their order of application. You can also disable project-level style sheets for a specific view.

Style sheets can be activated for:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activating style sheets' space='UAF2P']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activating style sheets' space='UAF2P']

##### Style sheets activation priority order

The order of priority in which the style sheets are activated for views:

1. The configurations for activating style sheets for individual views are of the highest priority. They are always of higher priority than the configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project.
  1. Take note of the type of style sheets you are using, i.e., whether [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating style sheets' space='UAF2P'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating style sheets' space='UAF2P']. Those that reuse another style sheet inherit the latter's rules. This can result in unexpected usage of styles even if their style sheets are not activated directly. ExampleYou have a custom style sheet, *NewStyleSheet**,* which is created by subclassifying the *DefaultStyleSheet*. The *NewStyleSheet*contains several rules slightly modifying the *DefaultStyleSheet.*You activate *NewStyle*for a specific view and *BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet* for the entire project.The specific view with the activated *NewStyleSheet*displays some symbols with the styles inherited from the *DefaultStyleSheet*, even though the latter is not activated for the project.
2. The ordering of style sheets for both individual views and the entire project is taken into account. If you specify multiple style sheets, order them based on your preferred priority, as the style sheets higher on the list take precedence over those lower on the list. You can specify multiple style sheets. Activating multiple style sheets is useful ifthe prioritized style sheet does not cover all available symbols. In that case, the secondary style sheet will cover those not covered by the first (if it contains the necessary rules).If you specify multiple style sheets, order them based on your preferred priority, as the style sheets higher on the list take precedence over those lower on the list.
3. The configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project is considered last.

##### Activating style sheets for the project

There must always be at least one style sheet activated for the project. If no style sheet is activated by the user, the default active project style sheet is *DefaultStyleSheet*.

###### Activating style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor

To activate style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing elements' space='UAF2P'] and open it in the Textual Editor.
2. In the new package's body, declare an individual part definition and subset DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::StyleConfiguration .
3. In the package's body, declare a part and define it by the part definition of the style sheet you want to activate for the project, then subset activeStyleSheets .
4. Click the**Synchronize**button.

packageProjectStyleConfig{ 
individualpartdefmyProjectStyleConfig:>DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::StyleConfiguration{

// *BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet* is activated as the primary style sheet for the project, and the *DefaultStyleSheet* as the secondary 
part:DS_Styles::StyleSheets::BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet:>activeStyleSheets; 
part:DS_Styles::StyleSheets::DefaultStyleSheet:>activeStyleSheets; 
} 
}

###### Activating style sheets for the project via the Style Sheets button

To activate style sheets for the project via the Style Sheets button

1. Click the Style Sheets button in the view toolbar. In the dropdown menu, click Project Style Sheets command. The Project Style Sheets dialog opens.
2. On the left side panel of the **Project Style Sheets**dialog, select the style sheet(s) you want to use and click the **Add [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button.
3. Select a style sheet on the right side of the panel and click the **Up**or **Down**buttons to change the selected style sheet's priority.
4. Click the OK button. The selected style sheets are activated and element symbols are updated on project view s. Activating style sheets for the project via the **Style Sheets** button automatically creates a new namespace *ProjectStyleConfig* with the active project style sheets configuration.

##### Activating style sheets for a specific view

To activate style sheets for a specific view, the view must be defined by a symbolic view definition.

- Symbolic views containing *Views::asInterconnectionDiagram* or *Views::asTreeDiagram* renderers, but [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating symbolic views' space='UAF2P'], are automatically defined by *DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv* once you [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activating style sheets' space='UAF2P'].
- If you are [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activating style sheets' space='UAF2P'], you must first [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating symbolic views' space='UAF2P'], e.g., *DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv.*

###### Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor

To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, p lace the cursor within the body of the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify.
2. Declare a part and define it by the needed style sheet, then subset *explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets*.
3. (Optional) To i gnore the specification of active project style sheets for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view:
  1. Declare a part, redefine *effectiveStyleSheets*and set it to *explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets*.
4. Click the**Synchronize**button.

view'General View':DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv{

// ignores active project style sheets for the view, activating only those specified to be active for this view:part :>> effectiveStyleSheets = explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets;

// activating *NewStyle1*and *NewStyle2* style sheets for this specific view:

part:NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1:>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets; 
part:NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2:>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets;

// alternative notation for active style sheet specification for a specific view (equivalent to the above):

part:>>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets= (NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1(),NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2()); 
}

###### Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button

To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button

1. Open the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify.
2. Click the Style Sheets button in the view toolbar . In the dropdown menu, click Apply Style Sheets command. The Apply Style Sheets dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of the **Apply****Style Sheets**dialog, select the style sheet(s) you want to use and click the **Add [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button.
4. Select a style sheet on the right side of the panel and click the **Up**or **Down**buttons to change the selected style sheet's priority.
5. (Optional) Select the check box for the Ignore Active Project Style Sheets option. Selecting the option ignores the specification of active project style sheets specified in the **Project Style Sheets** dialog for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view in the **Apply** Style Sheets dialog .
6. Click the OK button. The selected style sheets are activated, and element symbols are updated in the view .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Whether you're using predefined style sheets provided by the modeling tool or custom ones you've created, you can manage which style sheets are activated for the entire project and which for specific views. This flexibility allows you to combine multiple style sheets based on your modeling needs—for example, activating a global style sheet for the entire project while activating distinct style sheets for individual views. Additionally, style sheets activated at both the project and view levels can be prioritized by setting their order of application. You can also disable project-level style sheets for a specific view.</p><p>Style sheets can be activated for:</p><ul><li data-uuid="1a7b4cb1-a395-468d-a28f-3c6bfe18d92a"><ac:link ac:anchor="Activating style sheets for the project"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Entire project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Activating style sheets" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="42fc047e-3083-43f4-bc7d-e7ddcdbfbdf4"><ac:link ac:anchor="Activating style sheets for a specific view"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Individual view]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Activating style sheets" /></ac:link></li></ul><h3>Style sheets activation priority order</h3><p>The order of priority in which the style sheets are activated for views:</p><ol><li data-uuid="5665ad43-9516-4f9b-91d6-3ceca61e8e8d">The configurations for activating style sheets for individual views are of the highest priority. They are always of higher priority than the configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project.<ol><li data-uuid="0e8f922d-8f4c-42b7-b9f6-fe8c97287eb5"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Take note of the type of style sheets you are using, i.e., whether <ac:link ac:anchor="Reusing an existing style sheet"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[the style sheet is created by reusing another]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating a custom style sheet from scratch"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[created from scratch]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Those that reuse another style sheet inherit the latter's rules. This can result in unexpected usage of styles even if their style sheets are not activated directly. </span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b21f65c9-4f24-4fa5-9893-2da238f32992"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You have a custom style sheet, <em>NewStyleSheet</em><em>,</em> which is created by subclassifying the <em>DefaultStyleSheet</em>. The <em>NewStyleSheet </em>contains several rules slightly modifying the <em>DefaultStyleSheet.</em></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You activate <em>NewStyle </em>for a specific view and <em>BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet</em> for the entire project.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The specific view with the activated <em>NewStyleSheet </em>displays some symbols with the styles inherited from the <em>DefaultStyleSheet</em>, even though the latter is not activated for the project.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="43964a01-1c7e-40ce-825f-18ea4f557db3">The ordering of style sheets for both individual views and the entire project is taken into account. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">If you specify multiple style sheets, order them based on your preferred priority, as the style sheets higher on the list take precedence over those lower on the list.</span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef9e546f-f934-4040-8bf0-cba1b5f86c9b"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You can specify multiple style sheets. Activating multiple style sheets is useful if </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">the prioritized style sheet does not cover all available symbols. In that case, the secondary style sheet will cover those not covered by the first (if it contains the necessary rules). </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">If you specify multiple style sheets, order them based on your preferred priority, as the style sheets higher on the list take precedence over those lower on the list.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="bbb22561-d484-4359-b018-3cff96f7c5c8"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project is considered last.</span></li></ol><h3>Activating style sheets for the project</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3c82a63-fcef-44f0-bae2-6ec131bfd6a5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>There must always be at least one style sheet activated for the project. If no style sheet is activated by the user, the default active project style sheet is <em>DefaultStyleSheet</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Activating style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To activate style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="4611ee65-c3e8-4052-9cd3-fec43a986b67"><ac:link ac:anchor="Declaring a root namespace"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing elements" - version_number: 1
````
- version_date: `2025-12-30T12:29:03.033+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model customization > (2026x) Custom creation dialogs > (2026x) Custom View Creation dialog
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Active Create View Dialog allows you to manage all View Creation dialogs, determining which one should be activated for the project.Thepredefined *SysMLViewCreationDialog*configurationis activated by default for all SysML v2 projects. If you prefer using your custom-created View Creation dialog, you must activate itvia theActive Create View dialog.

Only one View Creation dialog configuration can be activated for a project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Manage Create View Dialog**command invokes the **Active Create View Dialog**, where the custom View Creation dialog's *CustomViewCreationDialog* configuration is activated for the project.

##### Activating the View Creation dialog

Once you activate a View Creation dialog configuration via the **Active Create View Dialog**, the active dialog's configuration is created in the model in a new root namespace.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

packageProjectViewCreationConfig{ 
partdefmyProjectViewCreationDialogConfig:>DS_UIComponents::CoreUIComponents::Dialogs::ViewCreationDialogConfiguration{ 
part:CustomViewCreationDialogs::NewCustomViewCreationDialog:>activeViewCreationDialog; 
} 
}

###### Activating the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

To activate the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the part definition element of a View Creation dialog configuration.
2. In the shortcut menu, click the**Manage Create View Dialog**command. The**Active Create View Dialog** opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

###### Activating the View Creation dialog via Project Options

To activate the View Creation dialog via Project Options

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space='UAF2P'] and in the General group, click **SysML v2**.
2. In the value column for the **Active View Creation Dialog**option, click the **Browse** button. The**Active Create View Dialog** opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button.
5. Click the**OK**button in the **Project Options** dialog. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093418 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00007: (2026x) Alias

- page_id: `298093418`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093418/2026x+Alias
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T14:33:52.669+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Element management > (2026x) Working with elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can specify aliases for elements within the context of a namespace. The elements may be owned members of the namespace or individual members of other packages. The alias name or short name can be different than the element's declared name or declared short name.

##### Specifying an alias

###### Specifying an alias via the Textual Editor

To specify an alias via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the alias, declare the keyword alias , followed by the alias short name and/or alias name, followed by the keyword for, and the name of the element for which you want to specify an alias.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space='UAF2P']
3. Click the Synchronize button.

package'Automobile'{ 
publicimportISQ::TorqueValue; 
privateimportScalarValues::*; 
partdefAutomobile; 
aliasCarforAutomobile{ 
doc/* Documentation of the alias. */// alias with documentation 
} 
alias<trq>TorqueforISQ::TorqueValue;// declared alias short name '<trq>' and declared alias name 'Torque' 
}

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093572 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00008: (2026x) Allocation

- page_id: `298093572`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093572/2026x+Allocation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:51:49.149+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Relations > (2026x) Traceability
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An allocation definition is a connection definition that specifies that some or all of the responsibility for realizing the intent of the source is allocated to the target, defining traceable links as a kind of mapping across the various structures and hierarchies of a system model, possibly prior to more rigorous specification. An allocation usage must allocate features that conform to the types of the ends of its allocation definition.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays a *torqueGenAlloc* allocation usage defined by the *LogicalToPhysical*allocation definition. The allocation allocates the *torqueGenerator*part usage to the *powerTrain* part usage. 
Additionally, the allocation connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P'].

##### Creating allocation usage and definition elements

###### Creating allocation usage elements via the Textual Editor

To create allocation usage elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed and unspecialized allocation usage, use the allocate keyword, followed by explicit related feature declarations or a comma-separated list of related features.
  2. For a named and/or specialized allocation usage, use the allocation keyword, specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space='UAF2P'] (optional), then the allocate keyword, followed by explicit related feature declarations or a comma-separated list of related features.
2. Click the Synchronize button. allocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // unnamed unspecialized allocation usage 
allocate(torqueGenerator,powerTrain); // unnamed unspecialized allocation usage allocating elements in a comma-separated listallocation : LogicalToPhysical allocate torqueGenerator to powerTrain; // unnamed specialized (feature typing) allocation usageallocationtorqueGenAllocallocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // named unspecialized allocation usageallocationtorqueGenAlloc:LogicalToPhysicalallocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // named specialized (feature typing) allocation usage// unnamed unspecialized allocation usage refined using a nested allocation for a more detailed decomposition of the containing allocation mapping: 
allocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain{ 
allocatetorqueGenerator.generateTorquetopowerTrain.engine.generateTorque; 
}

###### Creating allocation usage elements via the Create Relationship command

To create allocation usage elements via the Create Relationship command

1. Right-click the element you want to allocate.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relationship , then in the submenu, click Outgoing , and then in the submenu, click allocation . The Element Picker dialog opens.
3. In the Element Picker dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to allocate. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to allocate to and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to allocate to from the Containment tree onto the Element Picker .
4. In the Element Picker dialog, click OK .

###### Creating allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette

To create allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette

- To create an allocation definition:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **allocation** def button to create an allocation definition element.
  2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the allocation definition symbol.
- To create an allocation usage:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **allocation** button to create an allocation usage.
  2. In a view, click on the element symbol you want to allocate, then on the element you want to allocate to. An allocation connection symbol is created between the elements. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **allocation**or the **allocation****def**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.

###### Creating allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the allocation def keyword for an allocation definition.
2. Specify the element name, then open curly braces and specify the allocation definition features within the braces:
  1. Specify the allocation definition ends by declaring the end keyword, followed by the end name and/or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space='UAF2P'] of the needed element.
3. Click the Synchronize button. allocationdefLogicalToPhysical{ 
endlogical:LogicalElement; 
endphysical:PhysicalElement; 
}

###### Creating allocation definition elements via the Create Element command

To create allocation definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click allocation def for allocation definition.

###### Auto-creating allocation definition elements via the Extract Definition command

To auto-create an allocation definition element via the Extract Definition command

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage' space='UAF2P']page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">in a comma-separated list</span></span></span></span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// unnamed unspecialized allocation usage refined using a nested allocation for a more detailed decomposition of the containing allocation mapping</span></span>:<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">allocate</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">torqueGenerator</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">torqueGenerator</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">generateTorque</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">to</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating allocation usage elements <span>via the Create Relationship command</span></h4><p>To create allocation usage elements via the Create Relationship command</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the element you want to allocate.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Relationship</strong>, then in the submenu, click <strong>Outgoing</strong>, and then in the submenu, click <strong>allocation</strong>. The <strong>Element Picker </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Element Picker </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to allocate. Hold Shift to select multiple elements. </li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Allocation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to allocate to and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to allocate to from the Containment tree onto the <strong>Element Picker</strong>.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Element Picker</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><h4>Creating allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ul><li>To create an allocation definition:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>allocation </strong></span><strong>def </strong><span>button to create an allocation definition element.</span></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the allocation definition symbol.</li></ol></li><li>To create an allocation usage:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>allocation </strong></span><span>button to create an allocation usage.</span></li><li>In a view, click on the element symbol you want to allocate, then on the element you want to allocate to. An allocation connection symbol is created between the elements.<ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="769c3a3a-7b27-471d-b151-165748c95ab6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the <span><strong>allocation </strong></span>or the <span><strong>allocation </strong></span><strong>def </strong>button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li></ul><h4>Creating allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create allocation definition elements via the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the<span> </span><em>allocation def</em><span> keyword </span><span>for an allocation definition.</span></li><li>Specify the element name, then open curly braces and specify the allocation definition features within the braces:<ol><li>Specify the allocation definition ends by declaring the <em>end</em> keyword, followed by the end name and/or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Specializations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specialization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of the needed element. </li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c00fd817-e309-4d8e-839d-5b21596e9da9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">allocation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">LogicalToPhysical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">end</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">logical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">LogicalElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">end</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">physical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">PhysicalElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating allocation definition elements via the Create Element command</h4><p>To create allocation definition elements via the Create Element command</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click definition elements via the Extract Definition command </h4><p>To auto-create an allocation definition element via the Extract Definition command </p><hr /><ul><li><span><span <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298094146 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00009: (2026x) Analysis & Verification

- page_id: `298094146`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298094146/2026x+Analysis+Verification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:27:30.534+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section provides basic procedures for working with cases, analysis cases, verification cases, and calculations.

Cases, analysis cases, verification cases, and calculations are generally used within the context of model evaluation. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin Documentation' space='SYSML2EP'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSML2EP" ri:content-title="SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin Documentation" /></ac:link>.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span> </span></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298094154 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00010: (2026x) Analysis case

- page_id: `298094154`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298094154/2026x+Analysis+case
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:39:18.795+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Analysis & Verification
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An analysis case is a kind of case whose objective is to carry out an analysis on the subject of the case and return a result about it. The analysis result can be evaluated to determine whether it satisfies the analysis objective. An analysis case is comprised of a set of steps with an objective to evaluate a result about a subject of the analysis.

##### Creating analysis case usage and definition elements

###### Creating analysis case usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create analysis case usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *analysis def*for an analysis definition.
  2. *analysis* for an analysis usage.
2. Specify the element name.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases' space='UAF2P'] in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button. analysisdefFuelEconomyAnalysis{ // analysis definition 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
returnfuelEconomyResult:DistancePerVolumeValue; 
objectivefuelEconomyAnalysisObjective{ 
doc 
/*The objective of this analysis is to determine whether thesubject vehicle can satisfy the fuel economy requirement.*/ 
requirement:FuelEconomyRequirement; 
} 
// ... 
}analysisfuelEconomyAnalysis:FuelEconomyAnalysis;// analysis usage

###### Creating analysis case usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create analysis case usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. analysis def to create an analysis definition element.
  2. analysis to create an analysis usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases' space='UAF2P'] .

###### Auto-creating analysis case definition elements via the Extract Definition command

To auto-create analysis case definition elements via the Extract Definition command

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage' space='UAF2P']page.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=298093503 space=UAF2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00011: (2026x) Annotations

- page_id: `298093503`
- space_key: `UAF2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF2P/pages/298093503/2026x+Annotations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-12-11T09:14:57.250+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Common elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An annotating element is used to provide additional information about annotated elements. There are several kinds of annotating elements:

- comment is used to provide textual descriptions about other elements. Comments may be about the namespace that owns them, or they may be about different elements, in which case they must be specified.
- documentation is a kind of comment used to document the annotated element. Documentation comments always annotate a single element, which is their owning element.
- textual representation is an annotating element whose textual body provides a representation of the annotated element in a specifically named language (such as SysML textual notation). As for documentation, a textual representation is always owned by its represented element.

The annotated elements can own annotating elements, related via the owning membership relationship. The **comment** and **textual representation**elements can also be related to the annotated elements via the annotation relationship. In symbolic views, you can create or display the annotation and owning membership relationships using the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='UAF2P']** command between the annotating and annotated elements, which can be represented either as element symbols or compartment elements. The **documentation** element is always related via the owning membership, however, if it is documenting an element other than its owner, the relationship is represented in a view as an annotation.

For a line note in the Textual Editor, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing Textual Editor' space='UAF2P'] page.

##### Creating comments

###### Creating comments via the Textual Editor

To create comments via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed and/or unspecified comment, use symbols /* and */ with the comment text in between.
  2. For a named and/or specified comment, declare the keyword comment , specify its name (optional), then the keyword about followed by the name of the element it annotates (optional), specify the keyword locale followed by the language of the body text and the region and/or encoding, then symbols /* and */ with the comment text in between.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

/* May require revision */ // unnamed commentcommentaboutvehicle/* May require revision */ // unnamed comment about the element 'vehicle' 
commentrevisionsaboutvehicle/* May require revision */ // named comment 'revisions' about the element 'vehicle' commentrevisionsaboutvehiclelocale"en_US"/* May require revision */ // named comment 'revisions' about the element 'vehicle' with specified locale (in English, region US)

###### Creating comments via the Create Element command

To create comments via the Create Element command

1. Right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click**Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click comment .
3. Specify the comment text.
4. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space='UAF2P'] .

###### Creating comments via the view palette

To create comments via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the comment button.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the comment symbol.
3. Specify the comment text.
4. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space='UAF2P'] .

##### Specifying the commented element

###### Specifying the commented element via the Textual Editor

If the comment is an owned member of a namespace, then the explicit identification of annotated elements can be omitted, in which case the annotated element is implicitly the containing namespace.

To specify the commented element explicitly, use the *about* syntax. See the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space='UAF2P'].

commentaboutvehicle/* May require revision */ // unnamed comment about the element 'vehicle' 
commentrevisionsaboutvehicle/* May require revision */ // named comment 'revisions' about the element 'vehicle'

###### Specifying the commented element via the view palette

To specify the commented element via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the annotation button.
2. In the view pane, click on the comment element, then the element you want to annotate with the comment. The annotation relationship is created and the element is annotated with the comment.

###### Specifying the commented element via the Create Relationship command

To specify the commented element via the Create Relationship command

1. Right-click the comment element.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relationship , then in the submenu, click Outgoing , and then in the submenu, click annotation . The Element Picker dialog opens.
3. In the Element Picker dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to annotate. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to annotate and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to annotate from the Containment tree onto the Element Picker .
4. In the Element Picker dialog, click OK .

##### Creating documentation

###### Creating documentation via the Textual Editor

To create documentation via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword doc , then specify the name (optional), followed by symbols /* and */ with the documentation text in between.
2. Click the Synchronize button. parttank{ 
doc/* tank is full */ 
}

###### Creating documentation via the Create Element command

To create documentation via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element for which you want to create documentation, and in the shortcut menu, click**Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click documentation .
3. Specify the documentation text.

###### Creating documentation via the view palette

To create documentation via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the documentation button.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the documentation symbol.
3. Specify the documentation text.

###### Creating documentation via the Specification panel

To create documentation via the Specification panel

- See the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space='UAF2P'] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space='UAF2P'] page.

##### Creating textual representation

###### Creating textual representation via the Textual Editor

To create textual representation via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword rep , then specify the name (optional), then the keyword language with the language specified as a literal string, followed by symbols /* and */ with the documentation text in between. If the textual representation element has no name, the keyword *rep*can be omitted.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

partdefC{ 
attributex:Real; 
assertx_constraint{ 
repinOCLlanguage"ocl"/* self.x > 0.0 */ // named textual representation 'inOCL' for language 'ocl' 
} 
}

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An annotating element is used to provide additional information about annotated elements. There are several kinds of annotating elements:</p><ul><li><strong>comment</strong> is used to provide textual descriptions about other elements. Comments may be about the namespace that owns them, or they may be about different elements, in which case they must be specified.</li><li><strong>documentation</strong> is a kind of comment used to document the annotated element. Documentation comments always annotate a single element, which is their owning element.</li><li><strong>textual representation </strong>is an annotating element whose textual body provides a representation of the annotated element in a specifically named language (such as SysML textual notation). As for documentation, a textual representation is always owned by its represented element.</li></ul><p>The annotated elements can own annotating elements, related via the owning membership relationship. The <strong>comment</strong> and <strong>textual representation </strong>elements can also be related to the annotated elements via the annotation relationship. In symbolic views, you can create or display the annotation and owning membership relationships using the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display Others]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> command between the annotating and annotated elements, which can be represented either as element symbols or compartment elements. The <strong>documentation</strong> element is always related via the owning membership, however, if it is documenting an element other than its owner, the relationship is represented in a view as an annotation.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="598feaf4-cd19-40e6-b84b-7f12005cce91"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For a line note in the Textual Editor, see the <ac:link ac:anchor="Commenting in the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF2P" ri:content-title="Managing Textual Editor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Managing Textual Editor]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Creating comments</h3><h4>Creating comments via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create comments via the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and do one of the following: <ol><li>For an unnamed and/or unspecified comment, use symbols /* and */ with the comment text in between.</li><li>For a named and/or specified comment, declare the keyword <em>comment</em>, specify its name (optional), then the keyword <em>about </em>followed by the name of the element it annotates (optional), specify the keyword <em>locale</em> followed by the language of the body text and the region and/or encoding, then symbols /* and */ with the comment text in between.</li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a9cde6d7-6322-4532-ac51-4daaedb71db2"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span
````
